local_names.romanized_local: Aattu Saaps (Tamil romanized); Matan Chops (Hindi romanized)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has 'lamb chop food product' (FOODON_03302051); used as closest match for mutton chops. Mutton (adult sheep) is not separately coded; lamb chop is the nearest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers sheep meat broadly; no specific code for spiced/marinated mutton chops dish. Used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for mutton chops as a prepared dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of mutton chops in Singapore consists of approximately 2 chops weighing around 150g, commonly served as a main dish at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Chops — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Chops (cr_id G00010766). All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 5.2g/100g based on typical mutton fat composition (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 17174, lamb/mutton chop, cooked) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for spiced meat dish with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Chops as a prepared dish. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous spiced meat dishes with low carbohydrate content (~7.4g/100g) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The carbohydrate in this dish derives primarily from spice marinades and onion; protein (14.1g/100g) and fat (11.6g/100g) substantially blunt glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (30 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.35, rounded to 3.4 for 150g serving; reported as 1.7 per 100g equivalent. GL per 150g serving = (30 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg/100g — borderline high; potassium 210mg/100g insufficient to offset sodium load per DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake, request reduced-salt marinade preparation, and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or chutneys; the sodium-to-potassium ratio of approximately 2.2:1 is unfavourable for blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.4g/100g) with moderate fat (11.6g/100g); high protein (14.1g/100g) moderates glycaemic response but saturated fat may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton chops are conditionally suitable for Type 2 Diabetes due to low carbohydrate content and high protein that slows gastric emptying; however, individuals should monitor total fat intake, avoid sugary marinades or glazes, and prefer grilled over pan-fried preparation to minimise saturated fat exposure.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 5.2g/100g, cholesterol 85mg/100g, and sodium 467mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume mutton chops only occasionally, preferring grilled lean cuts with minimal added salt, as the combination of saturated fat, dietary cholesterol, and high sodium is unfavourable for LDL-cholesterol and blood pressure management per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; saturated fat ~5.2g/100g exceeds RA dietary thresholds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption including mutton chops due to high arachidonic acid content that amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine pathways; lean white meat or oily fish are preferred lower-inflammation protein alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Chops is a popular Indian-origin dish widely consumed in Singapore, consisting of bone-in or boneless mutton (adult sheep or goat) chops marinated in a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, ginger, and garlic, then grilled or pan-fried until cooked through. The dish delivers a robust, deeply spiced flavour with a slightly charred exterior and tender, juicy interior. It is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore as a main course at lunch or dinner, often accompanied by roti, rice, or a fresh salad. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 192 kcal, 14.1g protein, 11.6g fat (of which approximately 5.2g is saturated), and 7.4g carbohydrate with 4.7g sugar. Sodium is elevated at 467mg/100g, making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The high protein content and low carbohydrate load make it conditionally suitable for Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without sugary marinades. As a red meat dish, it is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its arachidonic acid content. It is generally considered halal and gluten-free in traditional preparation.

Mutton chops vs lamb chops nutrition

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Mutton Chops (cr_id G00010766) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest match (cooked lamb/mutton chop) and documented accordingly. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, clearly documented.
notes: trans_fat_g set to -1: trans fat data for this specific HPB Mutton Chops entry is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked mutton chops as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB Mutton Chops preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this dish as of 2026. Saturated fat (5.2g/100g), monounsaturated fat (4.1g/100g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.9g/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17174 (lamb/mutton loin chop, cooked, broiled) as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Chops exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros (protein_g 14.05, fat_g 11.58, carbohydrate_g 7.44) are non-zero and sourced from HPB.
